coredata.dc:title: Exploration of nitrogen-doped grape peels carbon dots for baicalin detection
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Xiaodan
Wang, Huiyong
Yu, Hongmei
Bui, Brian
Zhang, Wei
Wang, Shaoyan
Chen, Mingli
Yuan, Liqin
Hu, Zhizhi
Chen, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Baicalin, a flavonoid compound extracted from the rhizome of Scutellar iae Baicalensis, plays a vital role in improving liver function after injury, reducing liver disease and treating primary liver cancer, which is also the first SARS-CoV-2 3CLpro virus inhibitor according to the latest research data published in BioRxiv. Therefore, constructing a simple and highly sensitive analytical method for the determination of baicalin is of great significance for the clinical and pharmacy settings. Herein, for the first time, carbon dots are explored for baicalin detection. Using biomass waste grape peels as the organic carbon source, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots (PT-NCDs) were fabricated, which were synthesized perfectly by a simple, environmentally friendly and one-step solid-phase thermal method without adding any other organic or acid/base reagents. Based on the synergistic effect of photo-induced electron transfer and dynamic quenching, a quenched fluorescence sensor for the determination of baicalin with a good linear range of 0.1–20 μM and a satisfactory detection limit of 43.8 nM was constructed, which successfully quantified trace amount of baicalin in baicalin capsules, human serum and urine samples. The results indicate that PT-CDs are expected to become potential sensing materials for the real-time monitoring of baicalin in organisms, which is very important for our health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Grape peels
Baicalin
Photo-induced electron transfer
Dynamic quenching
Disease
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of baicalin in actual samples
Construction of baicalin fluorescence sensor based on PT-NCDs
Possible response mechanism of PT-NCDs to baicalin
Application of PT-NCDs in actual samples

University of Science and Technology Liaoning Talent Project
Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Fund

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial supports from the XingLiao Talent Project Grants (No. XLYC1902076 ), the University of Science and Technology Liaoning Talent Project Grants (No. 601010302 ), and Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Fund Project (No. LJKZ0300 ) are highly appreciated. We gratefully acknowledge the support from Ningbo Cuiying Chemical Technology Co., LTD. W. C. would like to thank the support from Solgro Inc., Guangxi Jialouyuan Medical Inc. and UT Arlington distinguished award.

Financial supports from the XingLiao Talent Project Grants (No. XLYC1902076), the University of Science and Technology Liaoning Talent Project Grants (No. 601010302), and Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Fund Project (No. LJKZ0300) are highly appreciated. We gratefully acknowledge the support from Ningbo Cuiying Chemical Technology Co. LTD. W. C. would like to thank the support from Solgro Inc. Guangxi Jialouyuan Medical Inc. and UT Arlington distinguished award.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM (inset: the particle size distribution histogram), (B) HRTEM, (C) XRD, and (D) FT-IR images of PT-NCDs.
XPS full spectrum (A) and high-resolution spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D) of the PT-NCDs.

(A) UV (pink line), maximum fluorescence excitation (blue line) and maximum emission spectra (red line) of PT-NCDs in aqueous solution. Inset: photographs of PT-NCDs solution under 365 nm UV light (left) and visible light (right); (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of PT-NCDs under different excitation wavelengths. Inset: normalized fluorescence spectra of PT-NCDs. (C PT-NCDs = 0.5 mg mL−1).

Effect of ionic strength (A), pH (B), the irradiation time with a 365 nm UV-lamp (C), and different oxidation time (D) on the fluorescence intensity of the PT-NCDs, Inset: H2O2 concentration. (C PT-NCDs = 0.5 mg mL−1).

(A) F/F0 of PT-NCDs to different metal ions and anions at the concentration of 50 μM; (B) F/F0 of PT-NCDs to the various amino acids and drugs (50 μM); (C) Fluorescence response (F/F0) of PT-NCDs to various amino acids and other drugs (50 μM, green bars) upon addition of 50 μM baicalin (pink bars); (D) Effect of incubation time with baicalin on fluorescence intensity of PT-NCDs. (C PT-NCDs = 0.5 mg mL−1).

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of PT-NCDs with addition of various concentrations of baicalin (0–70 μM); (B) Relationship between ΔF with the full concentration of baicalin. Inset: Linear relationship between ΔF with the concentration of baicalin (0.1–20.0 μM). (C PT-NCDs = 0.5 mg mL−1).

(A) The UV absorption of baicalin (pink), the excitation (blue) and emission spectra (red) of PT-NCDs; (B) Fluorescence lifetime of PT-NCDs in the absence and presence of baicalin (20 μM); (C) The CV curve of PT-NCDs, Inset: Band gap calculated from the UV spectrum of PT-NCDs; (D) The CV curve of baicalin (C baicalin = 0.005 M), Inset: E
g of baicalin. (C PT-NCDs = 0.5 mg mL−1).
(A) Electron transfer mechanism of PT-NCDs and baicalin; (B) the excitation (blue) and emission spectra (red) of baicalin.

(A) Fitting graph of the Stern-Volmer equation of F0/F versus baicalin concentration (0–20 μM) at different temperatures. (B) Influence of the different concentrations of baicalin on the UV absorption spectrum of PT-NCDs.
Synthesis process and detection mechanism of PT-CDs.
Tolerance of different amino acids and drug molecules for detecting of baicalin (C PT - NCDs = 0.5 mg mL−1, C baicalin = 10 μM).
Comparison of the sensitivity of different analytical methods for detecting baicalin.

K
SV , linear equation and correlation coefficient of Stern-Volmer equation of PT-NCDs-baicalin system at different temperatures.
Applications of PT-NCDs in serum and human urine samples for the determination of baicalin (n = 3, 95% confidence level).

head.ce:title: Exploration of nitrogen-doped grape peels carbon dots for baicalin detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The University of Texas at Arlington

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Baicalin, a flavonoid compound extracted from the rhizome of Scutellar iae Baicalensis, plays a vital role in improving liver function after injury, reducing liver disease and treating primary liver cancer, which is also the first SARS-CoV-2 3CLpro virus inhibitor according to the latest research data published in BioRxiv. Therefore, constructing a simple and highly sensitive analytical method for the determination of baicalin is of great significance for the clinical and pharmacy settings. Herein, for the first time, carbon dots are explored for baicalin detection. Using biomass waste grape peels as the organic carbon source, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots (PT-NCDs) were fabricated, which were synthesized perfectly by a simple, environmentally friendly and one-step solid-phase thermal method without adding any other organic or acid/base reagents. Based on the synergistic effect of photo-induced electron transfer and dynamic quenching, a quenched fluorescence sensor for the determination of baicalin with a good linear range of 0.1–20 μM and a satisfactory detection limit of 43.8 nM was constructed, which successfully quantified trace amount of baicalin in baicalin capsules, human serum and urine samples. The results indicate that PT-CDs are expected to become potential sensing materials for the real-time monitoring of baicalin in organisms, which is very important for our health.

ce:section.ce:para: Liver disease is the primary reason of disease and death worldwide [1,2], there are about 2 million deaths caused by liver cirrhosis and liver cancer each year, accounting for 3.5% of all deaths in the world [3,4]. Therefore, it is urgent to explore new methods and new drugs to effectively prevent the development of liver disease. Baicalin, 5,6-dihydroxyflavone 7-O-β-d-glucopyranuronic acid glycoside, is a flavonoid compound extracted from the rhizome of Scutellar iae Baicalensis. It has confirmed that baicalin has a variety of pharmacological activities, such as anti-viral [5], anti-inflammatory [6], anti-periodontal disease [7], anti-tumor [8], treatment of diabetes [9], neuroprotection [10], scavenging free radicals [11] and other characteristics. Because of the excellent antioxidant properties, inhibiting enzymes and regulating immunity of baicalin, it plays a vital role in improving liver function after injury, reducing liver disease and treating primary liver cancer [12]. The latest research data published in BioRxiv shows only 0.74 μg mL−1 baicalin can inhibit the new coronavirus, which is the first SARS-CoV-2 3CLpro virus inhibitor [13]. Therefore, the construction of a simple and highly sensitive analytical method for the determination of baicalin is of great significance in clinical and pharmaceutical applications.

At present, a variety of methods have been used for the analysis and detection of baicalin, such as thin layer chromatography [14], electrochemistry [15–17], capillary electrophoresis [18], high performance liquid chromatography [19,20], liquid phase chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [21], and so on. Although these methods possess satisfactory detection performances, they still display disadvantages, such as expensive instruments, time-consuming, complicated pretreatment, and unstable electrode signal, which limit their extensive applications to a certain extent. In contrast, the fluorescence has become the most potential alternative to these technologies due to its simplicity, low cost, high sensitivity, good stability, and fast response. Unfortunately, there are few literatures on the detection of baicalin by the fluorescence method. Jiang et al. [22] synthesized ZIF-8 doped with BPCDs by solvothermal method, and then prepared AgNCS/BPQDs/MOF enzyme-catalyzed biosensor assembling with silver nanoclusters (AgNCS). Based on the enzyme-catalyzed reaction, a dual-rate FL signal biosensor was constructed to detect baicalin. Although the method has a low detection limit, the process of preparing the biosensor is complicated. H2O2 with a strong oxidation and corrosive activity is used in the detection process, which limits its wide application. Liao et al. [23] prepared a conformation-locked diketocalix [3] carbazole and its hydrolyzate could specifically detect baicalin. Because the prepared fluorescent probe contains a carbazole structure, it is toxic to a certain extent. Hence, it is a realistic and challenging task to develop a fluorescent probe with simple and fast synthesis, high sensitivity, and not act as a pollutant while being a detector of baicalin.

With the increasing scarcity of earth resources and the severe destruction of ecological environments, the development of renewable resources for high value-added transformations and applications and the maintenance of the sustainable development in harmony with natural resources and the environment are the major directions of this current research. Biomass waste is rich in ash, protein and lignin, which is a renewable, environmentally friendly, abundant and harmless natural organic carbon source. It can effectively replace chemical reagents and fully meet the needs of environmental protection and sustainable development. Currently, CDs have been synthesized from shrimp shells [24], coconut husk [25], pomelo peels [26], bamboo leaves [27], wheat straws [28], coffee grounds [29], onion waste [30], lemon peel [31], and other biomass wastes [32–36]. At present, the fluorescence quantum yield of most biomass CDs is low, which is mostly less than 10% [26,27,29,30,32–36]. In addition, the fluorescence is mainly concentrated in the blue region. Furthermore, most of the preparation methods are hydrothermal method, which needs to be carried out in an autoclave with high pressures, and this method is not suitable for large-scale preparation of CDs. These shortcomings limit CDs’ applications in many areas. Wang et al. [37] prepared SO3H-ionic liquid with SO3H as a functional group, and the water-soluble biomass CDs were prepared by heat-treating cellulose with ionic liquid, while the quantum yield of CDs was only 4.7%. Studies have shown that introducing heteroatoms into the carbon atom framework can effectively adjust the energy state density of CDs, and enhance the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs [38]. Hu et al. [39] synthesize N and S co-doped CDs from the green tea residue using the method of high-temperature pyrolysis and concentrated H2SO4 oxidation. The QY of the synthesized CDs was up to 14.8%, which was more than 3 times than that of without element doping. Thus, exploiting high quantum yield and long wavelength emission CDs with a safe and large-scale synthetic method is urgently needed.

In the present work, PT-CDs were synthesized and were explored for baicalin detection as schematically illustrated in Scheme-1 . Biomass waste grape peels was the organic carbon source and urea was used to provide nitrogen, a simple, non-polluting one-step solid phase pyrolysis method was developed to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (PT-NCDs) with green emission for baicalin detection. Based on a synergistic effect of photo-induced electron transfer and dynamic quenching, the sensitive detection of the flavonoid baicalin was realized. It had been successfully explored to analyze quantitatively baicalin in baicalin capsules, urine and serum samples. Compared to the previously reported various methods, the fluorescence sensor constructed here is simple, sensitive and efficient, which has great application potential for detecting baicalin in clinics and drug monitoring.

The grapes were purchased from a local supermarket, peeled and dried. Urea, baicalin, quinine sulfate, amino acids and other drugs were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The reagents for the preparing of metal ion and anion solutions were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). The experiments were carried out in PBS buffer solution (Na2HPO4–NaH2PO4, 0.1 M, pH = 7.0). The standard stock solution of baicalin (0.005 M) was prepared with 1 M NaOH. All experimental reagents were of analytical grade and were used directly without further purification in the experiment. The experimental water was all ultrapure water prepared by the UPTA-30 purification system (18.2 MΩ, Jingwei, China).

The morphology, particle size and dispersion of PT-NCDs were measured by JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan), and the accelerating voltage was 200 KV. The X'Pert Powder X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, Netherlands) using Cu Kɑ as the radiation source with voltage and current at 40 kV and 40 mA was used for X-ray diffraction measurement of PT-NCDs. The element composition and ratio of PT-NCDs were obtained by recording X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The fluorescence performance of PT-NCDs was tested by an LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of PT-NCDs was recorded by Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of PT-NCDs was carried out on the Lambda 900 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The fluorescence decay curves of PT-NCDs with and without baicalin were recorded on the FluoroMax-4 TCSPC spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, USA). The Zeta potentials of PT-NCDs and baicalin were obtained with Nano series-ZS90 nm particle size potential analyzer (Malvern, UK). The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of PT-NCDs and baicalin were measured by an IVIUM electrochemical workstation (Vertex. One. EIS, The Netherlands) at a scanning speed of 50 mV s−1, which used platinum wire as counter electrode, calomel electrode as reference electrode, platinum carbon electrode as working electrode, and tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate as the electrolyte (0.01 M, dichloromethane).

The grape peels were ultrasonically cleaned twice with deionized water and alcohol respectively, dried and grounded into powder. 2.0 g of grape peel powders and 4.0 g of urea were mixed and transferred into a 50 mL crucible, and then heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the black product was dissolved ultrasonically in 20 mL deionized water, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove the insoluble and larger particles. The collected supernatant was dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500) for 48 h to remove unreacted raw materials and other impurities. Finally, the purified PT-NCDs were obtained after freeze-drying under vacuum at −50 °C for 48 h. A stock solution of PT-NCDs with 4 mg mL−1 was prepared, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for characterization and application.

Using quinine sulfate as a reference substance (quantum yield is 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4), the UV absorbance and fluorescence emission peak area of a series of concentrations of PT-NCDs and quinine sulfate were measured. The fluorescence quantum yield of PT-NCDs was calculated by fitting the absorbance with the fluorescence peak area according to Equation (1) [40]:
(1)
φ x
=
φ s
×
(
G r a
d x
G r a
d s
)
×
(
η x
η s
)
2
where subscripts x and s represent PT-NCDs and quinine sulfate, respectively; φ is the quantum yield; Grad denotes the slope corresponding to the integrated fluorescence area and absorbance; η is the refractive index of the solvent. For aqueous solutions, ƞ
x /ƞ
s ≈1.

In order to explore the sensing performance of PT-NCDs, 15 metal ion solutions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+ and Al3+), 13 anion solutions (CO3 2ˉ, Clˉ, SO4 2ˉ, Iˉ, Fˉ, Brˉ, CH3COOˉ, HSO4ˉ, NO2ˉ, HCO3ˉ, PO4 3ˉ, S2ˉ, SO3 2ˉ) and 14 amino acid solutions (Trp, Arg, Met, Thr, Glu, Leu, Gly, Val, His, Phe, Cys, Lys, Asp, Ser) were prepared with the concentration of 0.01 M. Nine drug solutions (L-Epicatechin, Quercetin, Puerarin, Naringin, Lcariin, Luteolin, Dopamine, Vitamin C, Baicalin) were made with the concentration of 0.005 M.

375 μL PT-NCDs solution (4 mg mL−1 in pH 7.0 PBS buffer solution) was added into a quartz cuvette. Then, the above-mentioned solutions including metal ions, anions, amino acids, and drugs molecules were mixed and diluted with ultra-pure water to the total volume of 3.0 mL. The final concentration of different kinds of substances was 50 μM. After incubating for 1 min at room temperature, the change of fluorescence intensity at 520 nm was observed to appraise the selectivity of PT-NCDs. Subsequently, baicalin was added into each mixed solution to evaluate the interference of coexisting substances on the quenching effect of baicalin. Simultaneously, fluorescence titration was used to probe the sensitivity of PT-NCDs to baicalin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of baicalin in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Serum and urine samples were taken from healthy adults in the local hospital. Baicalin capsules were purchased from a pharmacy. Before analysis, all samples needed to be filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane and diluted with ultrapure water to appropriate multiples. The spiked recovery experiment was used to verify the reliability and accuracy of the PT-NCDs fluorescence sensor for the detection of baicalin.

The particle size and morphology of PT-NCDs were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As described in Fig. 1 A, the nearly spherical PT-NCDs with uniform particle size and excellent dispersion were successfully achieved. The illustration showed that the particle size of PT-NCDs mainly distributed between 2.5 nm and 3.5 nm with an average particle size of 2.73 ± 0.59 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of PT-NCDs indicated (Fig. 1B) that PT-NCDs had remarkable crystalline characteristics with a lattice distance of 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (002) graphite lattice structure [24,41], which showed that PT-NCDs exhibited a certain degree of graphitization during the preparation process. From the XRD pattern in Fig. 1C, a broad and strong diffraction peak appeared at 24.8°, which was consistent with the (002) diffraction peak of graphitic carbon materials [42].

The surface functional groups of PT-NCDs were further characterized by FT-IR spectrometer and the results were displayed in Fig. 1D. The broad absorption bands at 3100-3500 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H bonds [26,29,40–42]. The peak at 2930 cm−1 was suggested as stretching vibration of C–H [28,40–43]. The peaks centered at 1705 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1 were caused by CO and CC groups [28,31,43]. In addition, the absorption peaks at 1453 cm−1, 1201 cm−1 and 1035 cm−1 were attributed to the vibrations of CN [28,41], C–O–C [41,44] and C–OH [29,43], respectively. From the above mentioned, it can be concluded that the surface of PT-NCDs is covered with an abundance of carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups, which provide CDs excellent hydrophilicity.

To confirm the conclusions obtained by FT-IR, the surface functional groups and the element composition of PT-NCDs were further identified by XPS. In the full XPS spectrum of PT-NCDs (Fig. 2 A), three characteristic peaks centered at 285.9 eV, 401.5 eV, and 533.1 eV were observed, which attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s with atomic ratios of 55.98%, 16.61%, and 27.42%, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) demonstrated four characteristic peaks, corresponding to CC/C–C (284.48 eV), C–N (286.02 eV), C–O/C–O–C (287.50 eV), and CO (288.82 eV) groups, respectively [28,30,45]. Fig. 2C displayed the N 1s spectrum, which could be divided into three peaks at 398.5 eV, 399.8 eV, and 401.08 eV, representing C–N–C, N-(C)3 and N–H groups, respectively [24,26]. The two peaks located at 531.25 eV and 532.5 eV in O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) were observed, which were consistent with the characteristic peaks of CO and C–OH/C–O–C groups [24,41,46,47]. From the results of XPS, it can be confirmed again that an abundance of amino and carboxyl groups is bonded to the surface of PT-NCDs.
The zeta potential of PT-NCDs measured by a potential analyzer was −18.03 ± 0.86 mV, which might be caused by the –COOH and –OH negative groups on the surface of PT-NCDs.

The optical properties of as-prepared PT-NCDs can be evaluated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. Fig. 3 A indicated the UV absorption spectrum (pink line) of PT-NCDs with a distinct absorption peak centered at 230 nm, which was ascribed to the π-π∗ electronic transitions of CC. A weak absorption peak around 283 nm was attributed to the n−π∗ transition of the conjugated CO/CN bond [27,48,49]. Under the optimal excitation wavelength of 440 nm (blue line), the maximum emission wavelength (red line) of PT-NCDs was located at 520 nm. The illustration of Fig. 3A showed that the PT-NCDs aqueous solution emitted bright green fluorescence under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (left), and it appeared transparent and brownish-yellow color (right) in visible light. Fig. 3B displayed a typical fluorescence spectrum property of CDs [49]. With the increase of excitation wavelength from 400 nm to 490 nm, the fluorescence emission spectrum of PT-NCDs were gradually red shifted (inset in Fig. 3B) and the fluorescence intensity demonstrated a trend of increasing at first and then decreasing. Under the excitation wavelength of 440 nm, the strongest fluorescence intensity was found at 520 nm. The excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior of PT-NCDs may be caused by the different size of CDs (quantum effect) and the distribution of different surface states caused by different organic groups on the surface [27,49,50]. The property of PT-NCDs is particularly suitable for optical biological imaging. Using quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 54%) as a reference, the QY of PT-NCDs was calculated to be 15.3%, which is equivalent to or better than previous reported fluorescent QY prepared from biomass waste [26,27,51–53].

The fluorescence stabilities of PT-NCDs are vital for the applications in chemical sensing. Fig. 4 A investigated the influence of different ion strength on the fluorescence intensity of PT-NCDs. Even the NaCl concentration was up to 1.5 M, the fluorescence intensity did not change significantly, which indicated the excellent salt tolerance of as-prepared PT-NCDs. As appeared in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of PT-NCDs was strong and stable at pH ranging from 5 to 9. When the pH was less than 5, it is easy to form hydrogen bonds due to the abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. The protonation of the carboxylate on the surface of PT-NCDs would induce the aggregation of CDs, which weakened the fluorescence intensity of PT-NCDs. When the pH was greater than 9, the deprotonate occurred between –COOH and –OH to destroy the surface functional groups of PT-NCDs, which resulted in a decrease of fluorescence intensity [54,55]. Therefore, the physiological environment (pH = 7) was chosen as the most suitable pH value for PT-NCDs detection. A neutral environment is very beneficial for the practical application of PT-NCDs in biological imaging or sensing. In order to explore the anti-photobleaching performance, PT-NCDs solution (Fig. 4C) was placed under a 365 nm UV lamp and continuously irradiated for 180 min. The fluorescence intensity only slightly decreased, which indicated that as-synthesized PT-NCDs had wonderful resistance to photobleaching. Meanwhile, to survey the oxidation resistance of PT-NCDs, the fluorescence intensity was measured within 120 min after adding H2O2 (200 μM) to the PT-NCDs solution (Fig. 4D). And the oxidation resistance was also studied by adding different concentrations of H2O2 ranging from 0 to 500 μM. The fluorescence intensity of PT-NCDs maintained a strong stability. In summary, the as-fabricated PT-NCDs fluorescence sensor has superior application potential during the analysis of complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of baicalin fluorescence sensor based on PT-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the outstanding optical properties, the application of PT-NCDs in the field of chemical sensing was further investigated. Fig. 5 A and B explored the selectivity of PT-NCDs to metal cations, anions, amino acids, and drugs which might exist in organisms and environmental systems. At the same concentration of 50 μM, Fe3+ and Hg2+ could slightly quench the fluorescence of PT-NCDs. However, compared with baicalin, their quenching effects on PT-NCDs were negligible. Meanwhile, other ions, amino acids and drugs had almost no effects on the fluorescence of CDs, which could be judged that PT-NCDs had a specific fluorescence response to baicalin. To study whether other coexisting substances could interfere with the quenching effect of baicalin, an investigation was conducted involving the fluorescence response F/F0 either with (pink bar) or without (green bar) baicalin (Fig. 5C). It is clearly shown that the influences of other interfering substances on the fluorescence quenching effect were negligible. At the same time, the interference of coexisting substances was further examined by increasing the concentrations of interfering substances with an error within ±5%. As shown in Table 1 , the maximum tolerable multiples of interfering substances were all high, which indicated that PT-NCDs could be used as a fluorescent sensing platform to specifically recognize baicalin. Fig. 5D displayed that the response of PT-NCDs to baicalin was very fast, and the fluorescence quenching could be completed within 1 min.

In order to explore the sensitivity of PT-NCDs to baicalin, the quenching degree of adding different concentrations of baicalin to the solution was evaluated. Fig. 6 A represented that the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm gradually decreased with the increase of the concentration of baicalin (0–70 μM), which indicated that PT-NCDs are sensitive to the concentration of baicalin. As described in Fig. 6B, ΔF (ΔF = F
0 –F, where F
0 and F represent the emission intensity of the PT-NCDs without and with baicalin, respectively) could not line with the concentrations of baicalin in the entire response range. However, when the concentration of baicalin was 0.1–20.0 μM (inset), there was a good linearity between ΔF and C baicalin (ΔF = 19.16C baicalin-1.439, R
2 = 0.9918). Based on 3σ/k, the lowest detection limit (LOD) for baicalin was estimated to be 43.8 nM. Where σ represents the standard deviation of 11 blank solution and k is the slope of the linear equation. At present, the analytical methods for the determination of baicalin mainly focus on electrochemical and HPLC. Compared with the reported methods (Table 2 ), although the electrochemical method has a relatively low detection limit, the preparation of electrode is complicated, and the signal is easily unstable due to the influence of the surrounding environment. The as-synthesized PT-NCDs from grape peels of kitchen waste are simply prepared, environmentally friendly, cheap in cost, and low in detection limit, which have greater application potential than the reported sensor system for detecting baicalin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of PT-NCDs to baicalin
ce:section.ce:para: At present, the fluorescence quenching mechanism based on CDs can be attributed to five main mechanisms, including photo-induced electron transfer (PET), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), internal filter effect (IFE), static quenching and dynamic quenching. Subsequently, the UV absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectrum, Zeta potential, cyclic voltammetry curve and fluorescence decay curve were exploited to discuss the fluorescence quenching mechanism of PT-NCDs to baicalin.

Firstly, comparing the UV absorption spectra of baicalin with the excitation and emission spectra of PT-NCDs (Fig. 7 A), it was found that there was no overlap between the spectral lines, which directly excluded fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and internal filtration effects (IFE) [56]. In addition, the Zeta potentials of PT-NCDs and baicalin (20 mM) in PBS buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.0) were measured to be −18.03 ± 0.86 mV and −14.23 ± 0.55 mV, respectively. PT-NCDs and baicalin were both negatively charged, the electrostatic attraction between them was weak, which confirmed again that the fluorescence quenching of PT-NCDs was not caused by FRET. Next, the fluorescence lifetime of PT-NCDs was detected by adding baicalin (20 μM) and without adding baicalin into PT-NCDs solution (Fig. 7B). According to the double exponential fluorescence decay formula, the fluorescence lifetime of PT-NCDs was estimated to be 4.26 ns and 3.62 ns without and with baicalin (20 μM), respectively. The obvious decay of the fluorescence lifetime indicates that the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) may cause the fluorescence quenching of PT-NCDs.

Next, cyclic voltammetry was utilized to characterize the electrochemical properties of PT-NCDs to further verify whether it was the fluorescence quenching caused by PET. According to the empirical Equations (2) and (3) [56,57], the highest occupied orbit (HOMO) energy level and the lowest unoccupied orbit (LUMO) energy level of PT-NCDs were calculated. Where E ox and E red represent the oxidation and reduction potential of PT-NCDs, respectively.
(2)
E
H O M O
= − e
(
E
o x
+ 4.4 )
(3)
E
L U M O
= − e
(
E
r e d
+ 4.4 )

Fig. 7C depicted that the E red of PT-NCDs was obtained to be −0.62 eV, which was brought into Eq. (3) to calculate the E LUMO of PT-NCDs as −3.78 eV. Due to the irreversibility of the oxidation process, the E HOMO of PT-NCDs could not be directly calculated by Eq. (2). Based on the UV spectra of PT-NCDs, the forbidden band gap (E
g ) was obtained to be 2.36 eV (inset), and then the E HOMO was calculated to be −6.14 eV by Eq. (4) [58]. The same method was used to assess the E red of baicalin of −0.48 eV (Fig. 7D) and the E
g of 3.07 eV (inset). The E LUMO and E HOMO of baicalin were −3.92 eV and −6.99 eV, respectively.
(4)
E
H O M O
=
E
L U M O
−
E g

It can be observed from Fig. 8 A that the excited state electrons of PT-NCDs can be transferred from the LUMO energy level to the LUMO energy level of baicalin, which confirms that photo-induced electron transfer (PET) causes the fluorescence quenching of PT-NCDs. Fig. 8B represented the excitation and emission spectra of baicalin, which provide the support for the proposed model.

Evidently, the fluorescence lifetime of the PT-NCDs solution changed significantly with and without 20 μM of baicalin (Fig. 7B), which was speculated that dynamic quenching might also occur. In order to verify whether dynamic quenching occurred, the relationship between the fluorescence quenching constant K
sv and temperature of the PT-NCDs-baicalin system at 298 K, 318 K and 328 K were discussed (Fig. 9 A). Table 3 demonstrated that the K sv of the PT-NCDs-baicalin system showed an upward trend with the increasing temperature, inferring that the collision between the molecule in the excited state of PT-CDs and the baicalin molecule enhanced and led to the quenching degree increasing, which affirmed that the quenching process was a dynamic quenching. The UV absorption spectra of different concentrations of baicalin (0–20 μM) added into the PT-NCDs solution (Fig. 9B) showed that there were no new absorption peaks, indicating that no new substances were formed. This also confirms that the quenching of PT-NCDs by baicalin conforms to the dynamic quenching mechanism. Consequently, the synergistic effect of photo-induced electron transfer and dynamic quenching causes the fluorescence quenching of PT-NCDs by baicalin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PT-NCDs in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: A spiked recovery experiment was employed to evaluate the feasibility of the PT-NCDs fluorescence sensor to detect baicalin in urine and serum of healthy person and baicalin capsules. Fluorescence analysis was performed under optimized conditions, as shown in Table 4 . Baicalin was not detected in urine and serum samples. By adding 5 μM, 10 μM and 15 μM baicalin respectively, the spiked recoveries was between 96.5% and 103.4%, and the RSD is less than 4%. At the same time, baicalin was detected in baicalin capsules by the same fluorescent sensors. After adding 5 μM and 10 μM baicalin, the recoveries of standard addition were between 96.9 and 103.3% with a lower and satisfactory RSD. It shows that the fluorescence method based on nitrogen-doped PT-NCDs for the determination of baicalin is accurate and feasible, and can be applied to the detection of baicalin in actual biological samples.

In summary, PT-CDs were tested for baicalin detection with a high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. From the perspective of environmental protection, renewable and sustainable development, based on the biomass waste grape peels as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen dopant, green fluorescence carbon dots (PT-NCDs) with a quantum yield of 15.3% was synthesized successfully through a simple, environmentally friendly and one-step solid-phase thermal method. The prepared PT-NCDs are relatively uniform in size, no agglomeration in aqueous solution, and excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, which endows PT-NCDs excellent application potential in biomedical imaging. Simultaneously, based on the synergistic effect of photo-induced electron transfer and dynamic quenching, PT-NCDs as a fluorescent sensing platform may achieve the sensitive detection of baicalin, which is a drug for treating liver diseases. There was a good linear relationship between ΔF and the concentration of baicalin ranging from 0.1 to 20 μM with a satisfactory detection limit of 43.8 nM, which is the most sensitive detection reported so far. The established fluorescence analysis method had been successfully used for the detection of baicalin in biological samples (urine and serum) and baicalin capsules, and the spiked recoveries were measured to be between 96.5% and 103.4%, which indicated that the construction of a simple, efficient, and sensitive fluorescence sensing method is expected to be used for real-time monitoring of baicalin in organisms, and has important practical significance in clinical diagnosis, medical testing, and environmental analysis.

Xiaodan Tang: Experimental design, synthesis and characterization; Huiyong Wang, Hongmei Yu, Brian Bui, Wei Zhang, Shaoyan Wang, Mingli Chen and Liqin Yuan: Measurements and Data analysis; Zhizhi Hu and Wei Chen: Conceptualization, experimental design, supervision and funding. Writing and editing: Xiaodan Tang, Wei Chen, and Brian Bui.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this article.

We gratefully acknowledge the support from Ningbo Cuiying Chemical Technology Co., LTD. W. C. would like to thank the support from Solgro Inc., Guangxi Jialouyuan Medical Inc. and UT Arlington distinguished award.
